name: Peter's confession at Caesarea Philippi
notes: A threefold synoptic account of Jesus questioning his disciples and Peter confessing him as the Christ. The shared core runs the same way in all three: the question of who men say Jesus is, the people's answers (John the Baptist, Elias, and the prophets), the sharper question to the disciples, Peter's confession, and the charge to tell no man. Matthew and Mark both name the setting near Caesarea Philippi, while Luke instead opens with Jesus alone praying, his disciples with him; these are complementary vantages on the same occasion. Peter's confession is given as 'Thou art the Christ' in Matthew and Mark and as 'The Christ of God' in Luke, all confessing the one Messiah. Matthew alone records the fuller confession 'the Son of the living God' and the Lord's answering blessing of Peter ('Thou art Peter ... the keys of the kingdom of heaven', 16:17-19), which has no synoptic parallel and reads in place.
